We note that the three galaxies with X-ray detections, namely A2-15753, C1-15182, and C1-19536 (see Section sec-agncont below), are undetected in Herschel while being detected at several in the MIPS 24 m band, f(AGN) _ MIR >40 (75 ) , 50 (80 ) , and 15 (60 ) , respectively, when adopting the 3 (1 ) upper limits in Herschel .

Assuming a power-law photon index = 1.9 nandrapounds94 , the X-ray luminosities L_ 2-10 keV are (6.4 1.7) 10 ^ 45 for C1-15182, (16.6 2.6) 10 ^ 45 for C1-19536 and (9.6 1.5) 10 ^ 45 erg s ^ 1 for A2-15753 (redshift uncertainties are included in quoted errors).

gmw_00007751.name: IGR J14552--5133
gmw_00007751.overview: IGR J14552--5133 is a relatively bright active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2207.02340] classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2207.02340, arXiv:1805.11314]. Its X-ray spectrum exhibits a soft excess component [arXiv:1805.11314] and a cutoff energy constrained to approximately 40 keV [arXiv:1805.11314].

sources.summary: The paper models IGR J14552--5133 as a bright NLS1/AGN whose X-ray spectra favor either low spin or a truncated disk around a rapidly spinning black hole.

quotes.quote: \ (IGR J14552--5133, LEDA 3076910 ; z=0.016 , Masetti06 ) is located toward the Galactic centre and viewed through a Galactic column density of 4.34 10^ 21 Willingale . The AGN is relatively bright and it was detected in the BAT survey Oh18 . Based on its optical emission line properties Masetti06 , it is formally defined as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1), however, its X-ray properties are less extreme than typical NLS1s (e.g. Waddell20, Waddell22, Grupe04a, Grupe04b ).

Alternatively, equally good fits can be achieved if the disc is truncated at 10 and the black hole is spinning at the Thorne limit ( a_*=0.998 ). In these cases, the model parameters are consistent with the interpretation that the corona is centrally located close to the black hole and illuminating the disc at a larger distance.
Our analysis suggests that \ might have a low or retrograde spinning black hole, but the more likely possibility is that the disc is truncated and the black hole spins rapidly.

sources.summary: The paper models WKK 4438 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 with soft X-ray spectrum, cutoff energy near 40 keV, weak reflection, and a lower coronal-temperature limit.

quotes.quote: We study X-ray spectra of four Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxies (NLS1s), Mrk 110, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, IGR J16185 5928, and WKK 4438, using Suzaku and NuSTAR data. The spectra of the four sources are reproduced with a model consisting of a cutoff power law component, a reflection component, and a soft excess component. The photon indices of all the sources are found to be 2 . The cutoff energies are constrained to be 40 keV for Mrk 110, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, and WKK 4438, whereas a lower limit of 155 keV is obtained for IGR J16185 5928. We find that the NLS1s in our sample have systematically softer spectra and lower cutoff energies than Broad Line Seyfert 1 galaxies reported in the literature.
The fits give kT_ e 10 - 20 keV for Mrk 110 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654, and lower limits of kT_ e > 18 keV for IGR J16185 5928 and kT_ e > 5 keV for WKK 4438.

gmw_00007752.name: DES-RG 399
gmw_00007752.overview: DES-RG 399 is a radio galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.39 [arXiv:1812.09997]. It is a star-forming galaxy that has been detected in multiple WISE bands and possesses NVSS and FIRST radio counterparts [arXiv:1812.09997].

sources.summary: The paper selects DES-RG 399 as a DES radio galaxy for IRAM CO follow-up, reporting WISE detections, z=0.39, and NVSS/FIRST radio counterparts and fluxes.

quotes.quote: Among the 50 sources we have selected two galaxies that we observed with the IRAM-30m telescope, as part of a pilot project to search for CO in distant star-forming galaxies. The two selected galaxies are both within the DES SN deep field number 3 and are denoted hereafter as DES-RG 399 and 708, where RG stands for radio galaxy. They have WISE counterparts in the allWISE source catalog with W4 magnitudes reported at 2.1 and 1.5 , respectively. In the other WISE W1, W2, and W3 channels the sources are detected at S/N >9.5 , with the exception of DES-RG 708 whose WISE W3 emission at 12 m in the observer frame is found at 2.5 . The two galaxies have spectroscopic redshifts z=0.39 and 0.61 , respectively.

We found NVSS counterparts for both DES-RG 399 and 708, at an angular separation of 1.2 arcsec and 2.4 arcsec, respectively. Their NVSS fluxes are (12.0 0.6) mJy and (36.0 1.5) mJy, respectively, while the FIRST fluxes are 12.54 mJy and 11.80 mJy.

gmw_00007753.name: DES-RG 708
gmw_00007753.overview: DES-RG 708 is a radio galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.61 [arXiv:1812.09997]. It has a WISE counterpart and may exhibit either an extended radio morphology or a two-hotspot structure typical of Fanaroff-Riley II sources [arXiv:1812.09997].

sources.summary: The paper selects DES-RG 708 for IRAM CO follow-up, gives WISE and spectroscopic-redshift information, and notes possible extended or two-hotspot FR II-like radio morphology.

quotes.quote: Among the 50 sources we have selected two galaxies that we observed with the IRAM-30m telescope, as part of a pilot project to search for CO in distant star-forming galaxies. The two selected galaxies are both within the DES SN deep field number 3 and are denoted hereafter as DES-RG 399 and 708, where RG stands for radio galaxy. They have WISE counterparts in the allWISE source catalog with W4 magnitudes reported at 2.1 and 1.5 , respectively. In the other WISE W1, W2, and W3 channels the sources are detected at S/N >9.5 , with the exception of DES-RG 708 whose WISE W3 emission at 12 m in the observer frame is found at 2.5 . The two galaxies have spectroscopic redshifts z=0.39 and 0.61 , respectively.

DES-RG 708 might have an extended radio morphology, unresolved by NVSS. Alternatively, it is also possible that the radio source has a radio morphology with two hot-spots, typical of FR II radio sources Fanaroff_Riley1974 . The available angular resolution does not allow us to distinguish between the two scenarios.

gmw_00007754.name: COSMOS-FRI 16
gmw_00007754.overview: COSMOS-FRI 16 is a radio galaxy that is classified as a star-forming system [arXiv:1812.09997]. This classification is supported by spectral energy distribution modeling of its far-infrared and ultraviolet emission [arXiv:1812.09997].

sources.summary: The paper treats COSMOS-FRI 16 as a target radio galaxy with marginal WISE W3/W4 detections, IR-to-UV photometry, 14.0 hr of IRAM observing, and star-forming SED evidence.

quotes.quote: COSMOS-FRI 31, in the W4 channel, and COSMOS-FRI 16, in both W3 and W4 channels, are only marginally detected by WISE, at S/N<2. We therefore converted the associated fluxes into 3 upper limits. In Table tab:COSMOS_FRI_photometry we summarize the IR-to-UV photometry of COSMOS-FRI 16, 31, and 70.

The five target sources were observed for an on-source observing time of 71 hr in total, distributed among the five sources as follows: 6.8 hr (DES-RG 399), 17.8 hr (DES-RG 708), 14.0 hr (COSMOS-FRI 16), 12.4 hr (COSMOS-FRI 31), and 20.1 hr (COSMOS-FRI 70). Sources COSMOS-FRI 16 and 31 were observed during August 17-22, 2016, in bad weather conditions. Observations were carried out with an average precipitation water vapor (pwv) value of 12 mm, as well as high average system temperatures T_ sys = 615 K and 898 K, for COSMOS-FRI 16 and 31, respectively.

COSMOS-FRI 16 and 31 are formally at the edge of the dividing line separating quiescent from star-forming galaxies, while COSMOS-FRI 70 is safely classified as a star-forming galaxy, according to the color-color plots. These results are consistent with those found by the SED modeling, which suggests that the three COSMOS-FRIs are indeed star forming, on the basis of their FIR and UV emission.

gmw_00007755.overview: 0500+630 is a radio galaxy identified with an 18-magnitude galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.290 [arXiv:2302.10214]. Although previously claimed to be a hybrid morphology radio source, its radio luminosity and the detection of a western terminal peak classify both of its lobes as Fanaroff-Riley II [arXiv:2302.10214].

sources.summary: The paper argues 0500+630 is not a HYMORS: VLASS reveals a western terminal peak, both lobes are FR II, and radio luminosity supports that classification.

quotes.quote: The first source is the radio galaxy 0500+630 (4C +63.07, J050526+630546), claimed to be a HYMORS identified with a 18-mag galaxy at z = 0.290 0.004 and having an flux density of 5.5 Jy at 178 MHz, whose VLA map at 1.665 MHz is displayed in Fig. \ 1 Saikia1996MNRAS.282..837S , together with its VLASS (3 GHz) map, both maps drawn to identical scales. A compact radio core is seen to coincide with the galaxy. A striking aspect of these maps is the enormous surface-brightness contrast between the two lobes, as originally highlighted by Saikia1996MNRAS.282..837S .

An unexpected outcome emerging from a comparison of the two maps shown in Fig. \ 1 is that the outer half of the western lobe is missing in the VLA map reported in Saikia1996MNRAS.282..837S and Saikia2022arXiv220605803S The missed-out outer part, which is captured in the VLASS map, in fact, does show a well-defined brightness peak near the extremity of the western lobe (Fig. \ 1). This peak can be readily identified as the terminal Mach disk of the western jet, testifying that, like its eastern counterpart, the jet feeding the western lobe too has remained collimated and supersonic until the tip of the lobe.

Tenability of FR classification Coming back to the radio galaxy 0500+630, its FR II classification inferred here is also in accord with its radio luminosity and optical spectrum. The source has a radio luminosity of 6 10^ 33 erg s ^ 1 Hz ^ 1 at 178 MHz which, as also mentioned by Saikia1996MNRAS.282..837S , is well above the luminosity ( P_c) which is supposed to divide the two FR classes.

gmw_00007756.name: J0715+5528
gmw_00007756.overview: J0715+5528 is a radio galaxy that was previously identified as a candidate hybrid morphology radio source [arXiv:2302.10214]. However, high-resolution imaging reveals that both of its lobes contain terminal hot spots, classifying it as a canonical Fanaroff-Riley II source [arXiv:2302.10214].

sources.summary: The paper says J0715+5528 was claimed as a HYMORS candidate from lower-quality survey maps, but VLASS imaging shows both lobes have terminal hot spots, making it canonical FR II.

quotes.quote: To further highlight this issue, we now consider the two sources, J0715+5528 and J1136-0329, recently claimed to be HYMORS candidates Kumari2022MNRAS.514.4290K , as mentioned in Sect. 3. The claim was made on the basis their radio maps from the NVSS Condon1998AJ....115.1693C and FIRST Becker1995ApJ...450..559B surveys. Redshift is available only for J1136-0328 ( z = 0.82 ) Kumari2022MNRAS.514.4290K . In Fig. \ 2 we present overlays of their Pan-STARRS-FIRST maps, reproduced from Kumari2022MNRAS.514.4290K , alongside their VLASS images in both contour and gray-scale representations, made with a 3.03^ 2.29^ beam at 3 GHz Lacy2020PASP..132c5001L . These VLASS images leave no doubt that in each source, both lobes contain a terminal hot spot, again demonstrating that both are canonical FR II sources and not HYMORS.

gmw_00007757.name: J1136-0329
gmw_00007757.overview: J1136-0329 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.82 [arXiv:2302.10214]. Although previously suggested as a hybrid morphology radio source candidate, high-resolution imaging reveals that both of its radio lobes contain terminal hot spots, classifying it as a canonical Fanaroff-Riley II source [arXiv:2302.10214].

sources.summary: The paper says J1136-0329 was claimed as a HYMORS candidate from lower-quality maps, but VLASS imaging shows both lobes have terminal hot spots, so it is canonical FR II.

quotes.quote: To further highlight this issue, we now consider the two sources, J0715+5528 and J1136-0329, recently claimed to be HYMORS candidates Kumari2022MNRAS.514.4290K , as mentioned in Sect. 3. The claim was made on the basis their radio maps from the NVSS Condon1998AJ....115.1693C and FIRST Becker1995ApJ...450..559B surveys. Redshift is available only for J1136-0328 ( z = 0.82 ) Kumari2022MNRAS.514.4290K . In Fig. \ 2 we present overlays of their Pan-STARRS-FIRST maps, reproduced from Kumari2022MNRAS.514.4290K , alongside their VLASS images in both contour and gray-scale representations, made with a 3.03^ 2.29^ beam at 3 GHz Lacy2020PASP..132c5001L . These VLASS images leave no doubt that in each source, both lobes contain a terminal hot spot, again demonstrating that both are canonical FR II sources and not HYMORS.

gmw_00007758.name: KKH 22
gmw_00007758.overview: KKH 22 is a nearby dwarf spheroidal galaxy located in the vicinity of the massive spiral galaxy IC 342 [arXiv:2005.03132]. Based on its distance of 3.12 Mpc and radial velocity, KKH 22 is likely gravitationally bound to IC 342 [arXiv:2005.03132].

sources.summary: The paper identifies KKH 22 as a nearby dwarf spheroidal around IC 342, measuring its TRGB distance, globular-cluster velocity, luminosity, surface brightness, and likely bound status.

quotes.quote: We present observations with the Advanced Camera for Surveys on the Hubble Space Telescope of the nearby dwarf spheroidal galaxy KKH 22 = LEDA 2807114 in the vicinity of the massive spiral galaxy IC 342. We derived its distance of 3.12 0.19 Mpc using the tip of red giant branch (TRGB) method. We also used the 6 m BTA spectroscopy to measure a heliocentric radial velocity of the globular cluster in KKH 22 to be +30 10 km s ^ 1 . The dSph galaxy KKH 22 has the V band absolute magnitude of --12 19 and the central surface brightness _ v,0 = 24.1 ^m/ . Both the velocity and the distance of KKH 22 are consistent with the dSph galaxy being gravitationally bound to IC 342.

gmw_00007759.name: KKH 34
gmw_00007759.overview: KKH 34 is a dwarf irregular galaxy with a radial velocity close to that of the IC 342 group [arXiv:2005.03132]. Based on a revised tip of the red giant branch distance of 7.28 Mpc, the galaxy is located behind the IC 342 group and near the Maffei group [arXiv:2005.03132].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies KKH 34 as a background dwarf irregular: new ACS TRGB work gives 7.28 Mpc, placing it behind the IC 342 group near Maffei.

quotes.quote: At a higher Galactic latitude, there is a dwarf irregular galaxy KKH 34= PGC 095594, with a radial velocity close to the IC 342 velocity. Based on a shallow CM-diagram, Karachentsev et al. (2003) determined the galaxy distance to be 4.61 Mpc. Since this distance estimation was considered to be unreliable, KKH 34 was included in our ongoing SNAP survey. An I band image from a new ACS observation is shown in the upper panel of Fig.6. The red giant branch with a TRGB position of I_ TIP = 25 60 is visible in the corresponding colour-magnitude diagram (bottom panel of Fig.6).

Taking the Galactic extinction of A_I = 0 361 into account, we obtain (m-M)_o = 29.31 0.11 or D = 7.28 0.36 Mpc for the galaxy distance module. Parameters for this galaxy are presented in the last row of Table 2. At this revised distance, KKH 34 is 4 Mpc behind the IC 342 group, but only 2 Mpc from the Maffei group (Anand et al. 2019a).

gmw_00007760.name: MS 2254.9-3712
gmw_00007760.overview: MS 2254.9-3712 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1411.0684] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1411.0684, arXiv:2009.03061]. It exhibits a coherent X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation with a timescale of approximately two hours [arXiv:1411.0684, arXiv:2009.03061], which originates from a narrow region of its accretion disk close to the supermassive black hole [arXiv:2009.03061].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MS 2254.9-3712 as a Seyfert galaxy with a coherent X-ray QPO, soft lag, possible harmonic, and hard energy dependence.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a 1.5 10^ 4 Hz ( 2 hr) X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) in the active galaxy , using a 70 ks observation. The QPO is significantly detected ( 3.3 ) in the 1.2 - 5.0 keV band only, connecting its origin with the primary X-ray power-law continuum. We detect a highly coherent soft lag between the 0.3 - 0.7 keV and 1.2 - 5.0 keV energy bands at the QPO frequency and at a frequency band in a 3:2 ratio, strongly suggesting the presence of a QPO harmonic. An iron K reverberation lag is found at the harmonic frequency, indicating the reflecting material subtends some angle to the primary continuum, which is modulated by the QPO mechanism. Frequency resolved spectroscopy reveals the QPO and harmonic to have a hard energy dependence.

We have presented an analysis of the energy dependent variability in the Seyfert 1 galaxy , based on a 70 ks observation. We found a significant ( 3.3 ) QPO at 1.5 10^ 4 Hz in the PSD of the 1.2-5.0 keV band. The QPO is coherent, Q 8 , and has an rms of 6 per cent.

sources.summary: MS 2254.9-3712 is treated as an active-galaxy QPO case; the model places its QPO emission in a narrow accretion-disk region, 10.4-11.4, close to the black hole.

quotes.quote: A QPO with 2 h timescale was detected in MS 2254.9-3712 .

We discuss our results below: We have computed the contours of _ , a , using Equation nuphicirc , for the QPO frequencies (given in Table AGNXrayQPO ) of RE J1034 + 396 (blue), MS 2254.9-3712 (red), and MCG-06-30-15 (magenta), shown in the , a plane in Figure plotra1QPO a. The masses of these black holes were assumed from the previous estimations (see Table AGNXrayQPO ). We see that the QPO emission originates from a very narrow region of the accretion disk, where 9.4-9.9 for RE J1034 + 396, 10.4-11.4 for MS 2254.9-3712, and 14.2 for MCG-06-30-15 even though a ranges from 0 to 1. This implies that the QPO emission region is very close to the black hole, and this emission region remains very narrow and nearly independent of the spin of the black hole.

gmw_00007761.name: SDSS J 135058.12+261855.2
gmw_00007761.overview: SDSS J 135058.12+261855.2 is a highly luminous, radio-quiet type 1 quasar located at a redshift of z=2.627 [arXiv:2203.15824]. It hosts a supermassive black hole and has exhibited strong X-ray variability, including a rapid dimming event [arXiv:2203.15824].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J 135058.12+261855.2 as a z=2.627 radio-quiet quasar with a luminous SMBH and Chandra-detected rapid X-ray dimming between weak and nominal states.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we report a dramatic fast X-ray dimming event in a z=2.627 radio-quiet type 1 quasar, SDSS J 135058.12+261855.2 (hereafter SDSS J 1350+2618 ). Its coordinates are _ J2000.0 = 13^ h 50^ m 58 12 and _ J2000.0 =+26 18 55 3 .

SDSS J 1350+2618 is a high-redshift ( z=2.627 ; Hewett2010 ) quasar recently identified as an unusual quasar that showed significantly weaker \ emission compared to the expectation from the _ OX - L_ 2500 relation Pu2020 . It is very luminous, with an absolute i band magnitude (normalized at z=2 ) of 27.79 , and it has a C iv based virial SMBH mass estimate of 6.3 10^ 9 M_ Shen2011 . Upon detailed investigation, we found that this quasar has been serendipitously observed three times by Chandra. It displayed strong \ variability, and there was also a state where it showed a nominal level of X-ray emission.

gmw_00007762.name: HCG 25b
gmw_00007762.overview: HCG 25b is an edge-on, transitioning galaxy [arXiv:1509.05779]. It is interacting strongly with HCG 25f, as evidenced by a connecting tidal tail and a polyaromatic hydrocarbon tidal feature [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 25b as an edge-on MOHEG transitioning galaxy, strongly interacting with HCG 25f through a tidal tail and PAH tidal feature.

quotes.quote: HCG 25b : Figure fig:hcg25 shows that HCG 25b is an edge-on galaxy. Deep optical imaging by eigenthaler+15 also showed that HCG 25b is interacting strongly with HCG 25f. A tidal tail connects the two, and the polyaromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) emission shows a small tidal feature. HCG 25b is also a MOHEG cluver+13 and is a transitioning galaxy in the IR (i.e., it lies in the optical red sequence as well as the Spitzer IR gap and the WISE IRTZ).

gmw_00007763.name: HCG 55c
gmw_00007763.overview: HCG 55c is a galaxy belonging to the VV 172/Arp 239 chain [arXiv:1509.05779]. It is located in both the optical green valley and the infrared transition zone, containing molecular gas morphologically classified as a disk [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper places HCG 55c in the VV 172/Arp 239 chain, reports resolved CARMA emission, no radio continuum or optical spectrum, transition-zone colors, and disk-like molecular gas.

quotes.quote: HCG 55c (Fig fig:hcg55 ) is part of the chain of galaxies VV 172/Arp 239 It contains a galaxy with a highly discrepant redshift (HCG 55e, v = 36880 km s ^ 1 ; sargent68 ). . HCG 55 is the most distant HCG that we have imaged with CARMA. The emission in this galaxy is resolved and spans approximately 3 beam widths. HCG 55c was not detected in 1.4 GHz or continuum, and does not have a published optical spectrum (to determine the presence of an AGN). However, HCG 55c is at the edge of the Spitzer gap, and is in both the optical green valley and the WISE IRTZ. The molecular gas in HCG 55c is morphologically classified as a disk.

gmw_00007764.name: HCG 57a
gmw_00007764.overview: HCG 57a, also known as NGC 3753, is an interacting galaxy in the Hickson 57 compact group that features a warped dust disc and a significantly warped, asymmetric stellar disc [arXiv:1607.02274]. It is classified as a molecular hydrogen emission galaxy with complex molecular gas dynamics and is currently interacting with HCG 57d [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 3753 as an interacting PRG-related galaxy in Hickson 57, with warped dust and stellar discs, asymmetric appearance, possible inner structure, and companions.

quotes.quote: NGC 3753 (UGC 6602, Arp 320, SPRC-203, PGC 36016) is an interacting galaxy, marked by as an object related to PRGs. It belongs to the compact group Hickson 57 Hick1982 and was included in the catalogue of nearby poor clusters of galaxies 1999AJ....118.2014W . The warped dust disc has apparent edge-on orientation. The stellar disc is significantly warped and has an asymmetric view. The galaxy image gives us a hint that its structure may include a bar or an inner disc overlapping with a dust component.
Two bright companions near NGC 3753 are the spiral galaxy NGC 3754 and the elliptical galaxy NGC 3750.

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 57a as a MOHEG with complex molecular gas dynamics, three kinematic components, interaction with HCG 57d, and mildly disrupted molecular gas.

quotes.quote: HCG 57a is found to have some of the most complex molecular gas dynamics in the sample, including three distinct kinematic components (Fig ref fig:hcg57 ), and is interacting with HCG 57d. HCG 57a is also a MOHEG cluver+13 , and an in-depth discussion of both HCG 57a and HCG 57d can be found in a14_hcg57 . The molecular gas in HCG 57a is morphologically classified as mildly disrupted.

gmw_00007765.name: HCG 57d
gmw_00007765.overview: HCG 57d is a companion galaxy that lacks warm molecular hydrogen coverage from the Spitzer IRS [arXiv:1509.05779]. The molecular gas within the galaxy is morphologically classified as a ring [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HCG 57d as a CO-imaged companion whose CARMA flux is below IRAM's, lacking Spitzer IRS warm-H2 coverage, and having ring-like molecular gas.

quotes.quote: HCG 57d, for which CARMA detected only half of the flux detected with the IRAM 30m (possibly due to resolving out flux, and sensitivity), is an outlier a14_hcg57 .

HCG 57d (Fig fig:hcg57 ) was not within the Spitzer IRS footprint, and thus we do not have warm H _2 information on this source. An in-depth discussion of both HCG 57a and HCG 57d can be found in a14_hcg57 . The molecular gas in HCG 57d is morphologically classified as a ring.

gmw_00007766.name: HCG 79a
gmw_00007766.overview: HCG 79a is a near edge-on, red early-type galaxy in the Seyfert's Sextet group that features a dust lane and a disk-like molecular gas morphology [arXiv:1509.05779]. The galaxy is optically classified as hosting an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.05779], which exhibits low-luminosity X-ray emission [arXiv:1204.0367].

sources.summary: The paper describes H79a as an optical galaxy in Seyfert's Sextet with multiwavelength nuclear activity, low-luminosity AGN-like X-ray emission, and a main detected count concentration.

quotes.quote: H79a and H79b show evidence for nuclear activity at radio, MIR and optical wavelengths with H79a a bone fide AGN and H79b a minor merger (D08); the X-ray sources associated with these two galaxies, even if coincident with the nuclear position, are at best very low luminosity AGN.
This suggests that the diffuse emission in HCG 79 involves two separate components: 1) the region of galaxy H79a and 2) the region of galaxy H79b and its surroundings, of about 100 counts each.

Most of the detected counts appear to be associated with individual galaxies (primarily H79a to the W and H79b and its surroundings to the E, cf. Table tab:1 and Fig. fig:2 left) and therefore it is unlikely that we have detected ``bona-fide'' diffuse group emission.

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 79a as a near edge-on red early-type member of Seyfert's Sextet with a dust lane, optical AGN classification, WISE IRTZ colors, and disk-like molecular gas.

quotes.quote: HCG 79a (Fig fig:hcg79 ), a member of Seyfert's Sextet seyfert51 although HCG 79c later identified to be a background galaxy , is a near edge-on red early-type galaxy with a prominent dust lane. HCG 79a is optically classified as having an AGN and is found in the WISE IRTZ. The molecular gas in HCG 79a is morphologically classified as a disk.

gmw_00007767.name: HCG 82b
gmw_00007767.overview: HCG 82b is a red-sequence galaxy that contains an optically identified active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.05779]. The galaxy features a stellar bar, and its molecular gas forms a warped, mildly disrupted disk that is not aligned with the bar [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 82b as a continuum-detected MOHEG with likely AGN continuum, red-sequence transition colors, a stellar bar, misaligned molecular gas, and a warped mildly disrupted disk.

quotes.quote: HCG 82b (Fig fig:hcg82 ) is the only CO-imaged HCG that was successfully detected in continuum but not in 1.4 GHz continuum. Despite this, there is an optically identified AGN martinez+10 , so the continuum is most likely due to an AGN. HCG 82b is a MOHEG cluver+13 located in the red sequence, as well as the Spitzer gap and the WISE IRTZ. Figure fig:hcg82 shows HCG 82b contains a stellar bar, where the molecular gas does not appear aligned with the bar. The lip seen at the edge of the molecular disk seems consistent with a warp, and thus the molecular gas in this galaxy is morphologically classified as mildly disrupted.

gmw_00007768.name: HCG 91a
gmw_00007768.overview: HCG 91a is a nearly face-on spiral galaxy classified as a Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.05779]. The galaxy is undergoing an interaction with HCG 91c, which has produced multiple tidal tails and offset gas velocities [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 91a as a blue, nearly face-on Seyfert spiral interacting with HCG 91c, with tidal tails, offset gas velocities, a blue CO wing, and AGN far-infrared contribution.

quotes.quote: HCG 91a (Fig fig:hcg91 ) is a nearly face-on spiral galaxy that is blue in optical and IR colors, with the CO(1--0) being brightest in the northeast quadrant of the galaxy. Deep H imaging from eigenthaler+15 seems to indicate that this might be the location of a bow shock. It is the only galaxy in this survey classified as a Seyfert 1 cluver+13 . The deep optical imaging of HCG 91a also indicates that it is undergoing a significant interaction with HCG 91c eigenthaler+15 , showing multiple tidal tails, including one connecting the two interacting galaxies.

vogt+15 also showed that the CO and optical line velocities were offset from that of the H i . HCG 91a has many different kinematic components but appears to have a blue-shifted line wing that runs directly north--to--south from the nucleus of the galaxy. DecompIR decompir suggests that up to 35 of the far-IR bitsakis+14 from the center is due to an AGN. The molecular gas in HCG 91a is morphologically classified as a spiral.

gmw_00007769.name: HCG 96c
gmw_00007769.overview: HCG 96c is an interacting galaxy [arXiv:1509.05779] that lies in the optical green valley [arXiv:1509.05779] and contains a disk-like molecular gas structure [arXiv:1509.05779]. The system exhibits star-forming infrared colors [arXiv:1509.05779], line ratios characteristic of HII regions [arXiv:1304.4493], and potentially hosts a radio-bright active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper treats HCG 96c as a low-mass Tully-Fisher outlier whose K-band luminosity may be boosted by recent star formation in a metal-rich system.

quotes.quote: HCG 96c As determined in 3.2, the oxygen abundance of HCG 96c is 12+log(O/H)=8.93 0.08. This value is slightly higher than the solar oxygen abundance given in Denicol o, Terlevich, Terlevich (2002). If we assume a solar or super solar metallicity and using starburst99 models, we find that for an instantaneous burst of a few million years, the B-band magnitude does not change much while the K-band magnitude can change up to one magnitude (as shown in Fig. mk_mb_49b ), supporting our observations. In fact, in a study of Seyfert galaxies and their companions, Laurikainen Moles (1988) found that HCG 96c has line ratios similar to those present in HII regions, finding also an over solar metallicity for this object.

Using the observed H luminosity, these authors suggested that a star-forming process is going on in this galaxy. They also concluded that the spectrum of this galaxy could be fit with models with a recent burst of star formation. In view of these results and Fig. mk_mb_49b , it is possible that HCG 96c has changed its K-band luminosity in almost one magnitude and, at the same time, keeping its B-band luminosity with the same value as before the burst (Fig. mk_mb_49b ).

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 96c as interacting with HCG 96a, lying in the optical green valley with star-forming infrared colors, possibly hosting a radio-bright AGN, and having disk-like molecular gas.

quotes.quote: HCG 96c (Fig fig:hcg96 ) is interacting with HCG 96a, as mentioned above, and appears in the optical green valley, and has Spitzer and WISE colors consistent with star-forming galaxies. The 1.4 GHz emission indicates that HCG 96c might contain a radio-bright AGN. HCG 96c also has a broad CO(1--0) spectrum, but that is possibly due to its edge-on orientation. The molecular gas in HCG 96c is morphologically classed as a disk.

gmw_00007770.name: HCG 100a
gmw_00007770.overview: HCG 100a is a star-forming spiral galaxy [arXiv:1509.05779] that contains an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.05779]. Although its continuum light shows a regular elliptical shape without spiral patterns [arXiv:astro-ph/0301075], it exhibits an asymmetric rotation curve [arXiv:astro-ph/0301075] and a rotating molecular disk [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: HCG 100a shows bipolar emission, regular continuum light, inconsistent isovelocities, and an asymmetric rotation curve that the authors link to strong interaction or accretion.

quotes.quote: The monochromatic image of HCG 100a shows bipolar emission regions but no emission is seen at the position of the continuum center, as mentioned by vil98 . The general morphology of the monochromatic map derived by vil98 is similar to that derived by us. Their map reaches an extension of 31", if we include the emission spot to the east of their map. This spot can also be seen on our map, at the same location and distance from the center (a radius of 19" along the major axis). Our continuum map shows a regular distribution of light, with an elliptical shape, and without any detected spiral pattern.

The velocity map shows that the extension to the east does not have a velocity gradient but, instead, it corresponds to a single isovelocity. We note that there is a variation of the position angle of the major axis along the radius of the galaxy. We also note three isovelocities which are inconsistent with the rest of the velocity field, located northwest and southeast of the galaxy. The RC for H100a is not symmetric, showing a flat part beyond a radius of 8 arcsec (2.8 kpc) on the receding side and a constantly rising velocity in the approaching side.

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 100a as a star-forming spiral with no obvious tidal tails, spectroscopic AGN evidence, mostly regular molecular rotation, and a possible bar- or outflow-related minor-axis component.

quotes.quote: HCG 100a (Fig fig:hcg100 ) exhibits prominent spiral structure, with prolific SF activity in its center (seen in optical light and PAH emission in Fig fig:hcg100 ). HCG 100a does not exhibit any outward signs of interaction, such as tidal tails, and is identified as star-forming in both optical and IR colors. HCG 100a is spectrally identified as containing an AGN martinez+10 , consistent with its detection in continuum and 1.4 GHz emission. The majority of the molecular gas in HCG 100a seems to be undergoing regular rotation, but there also seems to be a minor-axis component that includes higher velocities (seen as wings in the CO(1--0) spectrum), possibly due to a bar or an outflow. Despite the presence of a putative minor axis component of the molecular gas in HCG 100a, we morphologically classify this molecular gas as a disk.

gmw_00007771.name: A2146-B
gmw_00007771.overview: A2146-B is a bright radio galaxy and the brightest cluster galaxy of the primary subcluster in Abell 2146 [arXiv:1811.09708, arXiv:2204.05785]. It exhibits a strong absorption spectrum [arXiv:1508.01505] and retains a cool, surviving corona or stripped halo that is visible in X-ray observations [arXiv:2204.05785].

sources.summary: The paper identifies A2146-B as a bright radio galaxy in Abell 2146, measuring 144 MHz flux, average spectral index, a spectral break, and high-frequency steepening.

quotes.quote: A2146 is known to host two bright radio galaxies (namely A2146-A and A2146-B) that are located in the central regions of the NW and SW sub-clusters (see Fig. fig:hlres , left). One of these is the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) belonging to the SE sub-cluster e.g. Russell2011 . In projection, the radio galaxies are approximately coincident with the extended non-thermal emission from the ICM Hlavacek-Larrondo2017 and are situated behind the SE and NW merger shocks e.g. Russell2011 . The total flux density of these sources at 144 MHz is 75 11 mJy for A2146-A (i.e. the SE BCG) and 166 25 mJy for A2146-B.

We find that the average spectral index is 0.71 0.02 for A2146-A and 0.84 0.02 for A2146-B. However, the spectral energy distribution in Fig. fig:int_ps hints at possible spectral breaks at about 612 MHz and 1.5 GHz for A2146-A and A2146-B, respectively. To quantify this, we fit the radio flux density of the galaxies with a double power-law function. The resulting spectral indices are _ 144 MHz ^ 612 MHz =-0.53 0.11 and _ 612 MHz ^ 5 GHz =-0.79 0.05 for A2146-A and _ 144 MHz ^ 1.5 GHz =-0.64 0.06 and _ 1.5 GHz ^ 5 GHz =-1.17 0.10 for A2146-B, where in both cases the spectral index steepens at high frequencies.

sources.summary: The paper identifies BCG-B with a cool X-ray nib on Abell 2146's upstream shock and attributes temperature and Mach-number features to its stripped halo or surviving corona.

quotes.quote: The bright nib on the leading edge of the upstream shock is coincident with the primary cluster's brightest cluster galaxy (BCG-B). Gas clumps attached to and stripped from the massive galaxies in the primary cluster lie between the upstream shock and the end of the subcluster's tail. To the SW of the collision site, two further plumes extend perpendicular to the merger axis and parallel to the primary core remnant. These plumes are also visible in the raw image. Detailed maps of the gas temperature, metallicity, normalization and pressure were produced with a contour binning algorithm ( Sanders06 ). Contour binning generates spatial regions by grouping together neighbouring pixels with similar surface brightness. Pixels are added to each region until a specified signal-to-noise ratio is reached. The dimensions of the regions were restricted so that their length was at most two and a half times their width. For each observation, spectra were extracted from each contour binning region and appropriate responses were generated. To ensure sufficient counts, a single blank sky background for each dataset was extracted from an on-axis region with radius 1.5 . The spectra were grouped to ensure a minimum of 1 count per spectral channel. For each region, spectra from all observations were fit simultaneously over the energy range 0.5 - 7 in xspec v.12.11.1 ( Arnaud96 ) with an absorbed thermal plasma emission model ( phabs(apec) ; Smith01 ). X-ray spectral modelling of cluster plasma is particularly straightforward (for a review see e.g. Bohringer10 ). All radiative processes depend on the collision of an electron and an ion. Due to the low density, all excited ions radiatively de-excite before another collision occurs. The low density ensures all photons escape the cluster. Radiative transfer calculations are not required. Thermal plasma emission models, such as apec , are generated by summing over all electron ion collision rates. The collision rates are dependent on the temperature, electron density and ion density. The normalization of the spectrum is proportional to the electron and ion densities. The shape of the spectrum is determined by the electron temperature and heavy element abundances, typically measured from line emission. At temperatures above a few keV, the electron temperature is predominantly constrained by the exponential dropoff in the bremsstrahlung continuum shape at high energies. In contrast, the emission in a low energy band, such as 0.5 - 4 , is largely independent of temperature. We note that for gas temperatures below 3 , the prominence of Fe L emission at 1 provides a sensitive temperature diagnostic. The apec model therefore has three free parameters: temperature, metallicity and normalization. Additional parameters for the redshift and absorption were fixed to 0.234 and the Galactic value of column density n_ H =3.0 10^ 20 ( Kalberla05 ), respectively. This model assumes that the electrons are in thermal Maxwellian equilibrium and the ions are in thermal ionization equilibrium. While it is true that on microphysical scales kinetic effects and temperature anisotropies are important (as attested by lab experiments and heliospheric observations), on astronomical scales the fluid description is well-motivated. Kinetic simulations of shocks (e.g. Caprioli14 ; Park15 ) show that distributions are indeed Maxwellian when integrated on scales of hundreds of ion gyroradii ( > 10^ 12 for the ICM). For Chandra's CCD spectral resolution ( 100 ), differences in the electron and ion temperatures will not significantly affect the measured parameters. In future, X-ray microcalorimeters (onboard e.g. Athena, Nandra13 ) with spectral resolution of a few eV will detect thermal line broadening and thereby separately constrain the ion temperature ( Hitomi16 ). Similarly, detailed measurements of the intensity ratios of Fe K lines with X-ray microcalorimeters will be able to detect a non-equilibrium ionization state (e.g. Akahori10,Akahori12 ; Wong11 ). We therefore proceed with the absorbed thermal plasma emission model as described and determine the best-fit spectral model by minimizing the C-statistic ( Cash79 ). The best-fit spectral parameters were painted onto the contour bin regions to make maps in temperature and metallicity. Pseudo-pressure was generated by multiplying the temperature and square root of the normalization. For maps with S/N =32 (Fig. fig:maps ), the uncertainties are typically 15 in temperature, 8 in normalization and 18 in pressure. Regions at lower temperatures, such as the subcluster's core and tail, have smaller uncertainties in temperature at 5 and 10 , respectively. This improvement is due to the temperature sensitivity of the Fe L line emission, which is prominent at low X-ray temperatures. The uncertainties in temperature increase to 20 in the postshock gas at 8 - 10 . These higher temperatures are difficult to constrain because the exponential cut off in the bremsstrahlung continuum emission lies beyond Chandra's energy range. Fig. fig:maps shows the resulting maps of gas temperature and pressure. The pressure peaks in the subcluster's cool core and is elongated along the merger axis toward the upstream shock. The temperature map reveals the 2 gas along the leading edge of the subcluster's cool core and the steady increase to 8 through the ram pressure stripped tail. The gas behind each shock front has been heated to 10 . The primary cluster's core is visible as a relatively cool plume at 6 , which is 2 cooler than the surrounding gas. Similarly, the E plume is cooler ( 6 - 7 ) than the surrounding medium at 8 . The temperature map indicates a break in the E plume in a hot region at 9.5 covering roughly 6 10 . This region is coincident with a drop in the X-ray surface brightness. The parallel plumes also appear cooler than the ambient by 0.5 . The bright nib on the leading edge of the upstream shock, associated with BCG-B, appears cooler than the surrounding shock-heated gas by at least 5 . This is likely to be the remains of the massive galaxy's hot atmosphere, or corona, that has survived ram pressure stripping.

Emission from the galaxy atmospheres, particularly BCG-B, produces a clear artificial spike in the best-fit Mach number and postshock electron density from 30 to 55 . Comparison of the surface brightness profiles from the 30 - 55 and 55 - 80 sectors (Fig. fig:upstreamSB ), and examination of the residual image (Fig. fig:subshockmodel ), indicate that ram pressure stripped material from the BCG's halo is spread over at least 100 throughout this sector. Additional contributions from the halos of other large galaxies in the primary cluster are also likely (Fig. fig:image ). It was therefore not possible to cleanly mask out this emission in the 30 - 55 sector. The projected density model was a poor fit in this sector, ^ 2 =123 for 73 degrees of freedom compared to ^ 2 =68 for 73 degrees of freedom in the 55 - 80 sector (Fig. fig:upstreamSB ). The galaxy halo of BCG-B is also detected as a cool patch in the middle of the upstream shock front in the temperature map (Fig. fig:maps ).

sources.summary: The paper uses the A2146-B BCG as one of two velocity and position tracers in its two-body merger model and notes its strong absorption spectrum with well-defined features.

quotes.quote: We therefore opt to use the difference in radial velocities of the two BCGs in each of the subclusters as a determination of V_ R . The spectra of the two BCGs are shown in Section 3 (Fig. BCG_b ). The BCG in A2146-B has a strong absorption spectrum with well defined features, as is typical of BCGs.
R_ P is taken as the projected separation of the two BCGS. At the cluster redshift this corresponds to 0.69 Mpc.

gmw_00007772.name: J023658+024217
gmw_00007772.overview: J023658+024217 is an Sb spiral galaxy characterized by a bright nucleus [arXiv:1404.7218]. It resides in a close group with several other galaxies of likely similar redshift, and it may feature double absorption lines [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper describes J023658+024217 as a bright-nucleus Sb spiral in a likely same-redshift group, with possible double absorption lines and blue-component SDSS redshift.

quotes.quote: J023658+024217: an Sb spiral with a bright nucleus. In close group with several galaxies whose appearance suggest similar redshift. The absorption lines could also be double, but this needs confirmation with higher s/n. The SDSS redshift corresponds to the velocity of the blue NEL component.

gmw_00007773.name: J023832+023349
gmw_00007773.overview: J023832+023349 is a broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy that is likely of the SBa morphological type [arXiv:1404.7218]. Its spectrum displays double-peaked broad emission lines with profile changes over time, as well as double peaks on all narrow emission lines [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J023832+023349 as a broad-line Seyfert 1 with double-peaked broad and narrow lines, internal extinction, and prior profile changes challenging a simple disk model.

quotes.quote: J023832+023349, (PKS 0235+023), is a broad line Seyfert 1 galaxy, probably of SBa type, whose \ broad line exhibits double peaks or twin shoulders. This was fitted quite well, on 1991 observations, with a model attributing the broad emission to a circular, relativistic, Keplerian disk (Eracleous Halpern ). However, Gezari et al. ( ) presented a long-term monitoring of the double-peaked broad \ emission lines and found a dramatic change in profile shape after 1991 leading to consider the simple circular disk model as insufficient for interpretation of the spectra taken after 1991.

The present LAMOST spectrum not only shows a double peak on \ broad component, but also double peaks on all narrow lines including [ OII ] , [ NeIII ] and [ NeV ] as well as the Balmer series. The broad component seems highly obscured by internal extinction, since it is almost invisible in . It is clear that this object deserves detailed follow-up observations.

gmw_00007774.name: J091646+283526
gmw_00007774.overview: J091646+283526 is an amorphous galaxy with a perturbed morphology and a bright nucleus located near a second, redder nucleus [arXiv:1404.7218]. Classified as a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy, this strong merger candidate exhibits broad Balmer emission lines and an extended radio structure [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper describes J091646+283526 as a perturbed amorphous Seyfert-like system with a nearby redder nucleus, broad Balmer emission, strong excitation, merger candidacy, and extended radio structure.

quotes.quote: J091646+283526 is a quite amorphous galaxy with a perturbed morphology. The bright nucleus is close (2-3'') to a second, much redder one. A moderately broad component is present in the Balmer emission lines.

The excitation is very strong with bright HeII and [ NeV ] lines (Seyfert 1.5). This object is a very good merger candidate. The NVSS map shows an extended source with two components, one being coincident with the optical object.

gmw_00007775.name: J121342+422202
gmw_00007775.overview: J121342+422202 is a galaxy characterized by a distended, asymmetric envelope [arXiv:1404.7218]. It is a member of a group containing more than ten objects of similar redshift and is interpreted as a possible merger or post-merger [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper describes J121342+422202 as an asymmetric-envelope galaxy near a diffuse object, embedded in a similar-redshift group, and interpreted as a possible merger or post-merger.

quotes.quote: J121342+422202: a galaxy with a distended asymmetric envelope. The \ range is lacking on the SDSS spectrum. A small diffuse object is very close. The galaxy is member of a group of more than 10 objects of similar redshift, among which a Seyfert 1 at 4.1 arcmin N. Possible merger or post-merger.

gmw_00007776.name: J131434+563419
gmw_00007776.overview: J131434+563419 is a peculiar, asymmetric galaxy that is possibly barred [arXiv:1404.7218]. It features a blue extension that may be a tidal tail, suggesting it is a merger candidate within a loose group of galaxies [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper describes J131434+563419 as a peculiar, possibly barred asymmetric galaxy with a blue tidal-tail candidate and a possible merger interpretation in a loose same-redshift group.

quotes.quote: J131434+563419: peculiar asymmetric-shaped, possibly barred, galaxy with a blue extension that could be a tidal tail. Possible merger candidate. In a loose group with other, similar redshift, galaxies.

gmw_00007777.name: J135207+052555
gmw_00007777.overview: J135207+052555 is a luminous early-type Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:1404.7218]. It is a member of the Abell 1809 galaxy cluster and features a single resolved nucleus with triple-peaked narrow emission lines [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J135207+052555 as a luminous early-type Seyfert 2 with a single SDSS-resolved nucleus, triple-peaked narrow lines, and membership in Abell 1809.

quotes.quote: J135207+052555: typical luminous Seyfert 2 galaxy, of early-type (SO or SO/a). The nucleus appears unique at the spatial resolution of SDSS image. The double-lined character of the NEL is especially obvious on the \ lines which appear as a triple peak. This object is a member of the Abell 1809 galaxy cluster.

gmw_00007778.name: J140225+465817
gmw_00007778.overview: J140225+465817 is an isolated galaxy that exhibits a perturbed morphology [arXiv:1404.7218]. It features a northeastern extension or projected close companion, along with irregular fuzzy structures to the south, suggesting it is a possible merger candidate [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper describes J140225+465817 as morphologically perturbed, with a possible companion or extension, southern irregular fuzz, and a possible merger interpretation despite isolation.

quotes.quote: J140225+465817: a galaxy with perturbed morphology, with an extension (or projected close companion) along NE and irregular fuzzy extension along S. Possible merger candidate. Isolated.

gmw_00007779.name: J232703+004256
gmw_00007779.overview: J232703+004256 is an amorphous galaxy that forms an interacting pair with a companion galaxy located 25 arcseconds to its northeast [arXiv:1404.7218]. The system features a single resolved nucleus, a diffuse matter bridge connecting the two galaxies, and a tidal tail extending toward the southwest [arXiv:1404.7218].

sources.summary: The paper describes J232703+004256 as an amorphous galaxy in an interacting pair, with a diffuse bridge, a southwest tidal tail, and one SDSS-resolved nucleus.

quotes.quote: J232703+004256: an amorphous galaxy (maybe Sa), forming an interactive pair with a companion at 25 arcsec NE, of similar size and brightness. A diffuse matter bridge is seen between both objects, and a tidal tail appears towards SW. Single nucleus at SDSS resolution.

gmw_00007780.name: SMM J13120+4242
gmw_00007780.overview: SMM J13120+4242 is an unlensed submillimeter galaxy at a redshift of approximately 3.4 [arXiv:2203.15811, arXiv:astro-ph/0607089]. It is in an advanced merger stage [arXiv:2203.15811, arXiv:1009.2495] and is undergoing a rapid transitional phase from a merger-driven starburst to an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2203.15811].

sources.summary: The paper studies SMM J13120+4242 as a z=3.4 unlensed submillimeter galaxy with extended turbulent CO gas, advanced-merger morphology, central AGN evidence, and a transitional starburst-to-quasar phase.

quotes.quote: We present a high-resolution study of the cold molecular gas as traced by CO(1-0) in the unlensed z 3.4 submillimeter galaxy SMM J13120+4242, using multi-configuration observations with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (JVLA). The gas reservoir, imaged on 0.39 " ( 3 kpc) scales, is resolved into two components separated by 11 kpc with a total extent of 16 3 kpc. Despite the large spatial extent of the reservoir, the observations show a CO(1-0) FWHM linewidth of only 267 64 km s ^ 1 .

Despite the presence of a velocity gradient (consistent with previous resolved CO(6-5) imaging), the CO(1-0) imaging shows evidence for significant turbulent motions which are preventing the gas from fully settling into a disk. The system likely represents a merger in an advanced stage. Although the dynamical mass is highly uncertain, we use it to place an upper limit on the CO–to–H _2 mass conversion factor _ CO of 1.4.

Based on the low gas fraction, short gas depletion time and evidence for a central AGN, we propose that SMM J13120 is in a rapid transitional phase between a merger-driven starburst and an unobscured quasar. The case of SMM J13120 highlights the how mergers may drive important physical changes in galaxies without pushing them off the main sequence.

sources.summary: The paper reports CO(1-0) emission from SMM J13120+4242 at z=3.408, measuring a broad line, sub-thermal gas excitation, and a very large cold molecular gas mass.

quotes.quote: We report the first detection of CO(1 0) emission from a submillimeter-selected galaxy, using the Green Bank Telescope. We identify the line in the spectrum of SMM J13120+4242 as a broad emission feature at z=3.408 , with V_ FWHM = 1040 190 . If the observed CO(1 0) line profile arises from a single object and not several merging objects, then the CO(4 3)/CO(1 0) brightness temperature ratio of 0.26 suggests n( ) > 3-10 10^ 2 cm ^ 3 and the presence of sub-thermally excited gas. The integrated line flux implies a cold molecular gas mass M( )=1.6 10^ 11 , comparable to the dynamical mass estimate and 4 times larger than the H _ 2 mass predicted from the CO(4 3) line assuming a brightness temperature ratio of 1.0. While our observations confirm that this submillimeter galaxy is massive and gas-rich, they also suggest that extrapolating gas masses from J_ upper 3 transitions of CO leads to considerable uncertainties.

sources.summary: The paper describes SMM J131201 as a high-redshift SMG with complex spatial and velocity structure, no ordered rotation, and evidence favoring an advanced pre-coalescence merger stage.

quotes.quote: SMM J131201 (z = 3.41) -- This system shows complex spatial and velocity distributions. There is no evidence of ordered rotation, although the extensions to the east and north-east are redshifted with respect to the emission peak. The substructures, which may be clumps, outflows, or tidal tails, and the overall lack of rotation most likely point towards an advanced, pre-coalescence merger stage. This conclusion is also drawn by .

sources.summary: The paper singles out SMMJ131201.17+424208.1 among extreme SMGs with very high SSFRs, hot dust, most stellar mass formed in the current starburst, and likely major-merger origins.

quotes.quote: At the extreme there are three high- z SMGs SMMJ131201.17+424208.1, SMMJ141802.87+523011.1, SMMJ221806.77+001245.7 plus a low-mass, low- z case, SMMJ030238.62+001106.3 with very high SSFRs >10 Gyr ^ 1 (Column of Table tab:grasilres ). They are all hot ( T_d>60 K, Column ) and formed the majority of their stellar populations during the ongoing starburst ( M_ burst /M_*>60 , Column ). Therefore they are likely the most powerful cases of SMGs formed in major mergers of galaxies with huge gas reservoirs that were subsequently converted into stars.

gmw_00007781.name: J0213--0626
gmw_00007781.overview: J0213--0626 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy [arXiv:2404.15413]. Observations of the galaxy with ALMA revealed a marginal, low-significance detection of [C II] line emission [arXiv:2404.15413].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0213--0626 as a marginal [ C 2 ] detection with low-significance emission, aperture-based line flux recovery, and exclusion from the main ALMA kinematic subset.

quotes.quote: Out of 31 targeted objects, only four quasars (J0829+4117, J0839+3900, J1129+1846, and J1216+4519) are undetected in [ C 2 ] and two quasars have marginal line detections at a 2.6 level (J0213 0626) and a 2.1 level (J0837+4929), respectively.

We successfully fit all the detected [ C 2 ] emission except for J0213 0626 because of the low significance of its detection. Since the [ C 2 ] maps are created by integrating over 1.4 _ line , which encloses 83 of the total line flux for a Gaussian profile, we take this factor into account when computing the total line flux see also Decarli18 . The line flux and the uncertainty measured from the 2D fitting are also listed in Table tbl:c2fit . For J0213--0626, we use the line flux calculated from the intensity map with a 1 5 diameter aperture after correcting for the missing flux.
Three objects observed with ALMA (J0213--0626, J0319--1008, and J1129+1846) have relatively low significance detections, hence we only focus on the 23 objects observed with ALMA.

gmw_00007782.name: J0829+4117
gmw_00007782.overview: J0829+4117 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy [arXiv:2404.15413]. Observations of the system with the Northern Extended Millimeter Array did not detect ionized carbon emission or a far-infrared dust continuum [arXiv:2404.15413].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0829+4117 as one of the [ C 2 ] undetected NOEMA quasars, observed in shallower data and lacking a FIR continuum detection.

quotes.quote: Out of 31 targeted objects, only four quasars (J0829+4117, J0839+3900, J1129+1846, and J1216+4519) are undetected in [ C 2 ] and two quasars have marginal line detections at a 2.6 level (J0213 0626) and a 2.1 level (J0837+4929), respectively. Three (J0829+4117, J0839+3900, and J1216+4519) out of the four [ C 2 ] undetected quasars are from the NOEMA observations, which have a depth that is two times shallower than that of the ALMA observations. The FIR continuum flux and flux uncertainty measured from the 1D spectral fitting are also listed in Table tbl:c2fit . All objects observed with ALMA, except for J1129+1846, are detected in the dust continuum at high significance. Similar to the [ C 2 ] results, the FIR continua of J0829+4117, J0839+3900, and J1216+4519 are not detected by NOEMA.

gmw_00007783.name: J1129+1846
gmw_00007783.overview: J1129+1846 is a radio-loud quasar located at a high redshift of z=6.82 [arXiv:2309.03936, arXiv:2404.15413]. The galaxy is intrinsically faint in both [C II] and far-infrared dust continuum emissions, and it is classified as a gigahertz peaked spectrum source [arXiv:2309.03936, arXiv:2404.15413].

sources.summary: The paper models J172+18 as a z=6.82 radio-loud quasar with tentative low-frequency detection, likely peaked spectrum, and a rest-frame turnover between 2 and 10 GHz.

quotes.quote: One of the highest redshift radio-loud quasar to date is J172+18 at z=6.82 , which was discovered by . It has been observed previously in the VLA-S and -L band Banados2021ApJ...909...80B and the VLBA at 1.53, 4.67, and 7.67 GHz Momjian2021AJ....161..207M . The VLA-S and L-band observations show a steep negative spectral index of =-1.31 0.08 , but the VLBA observations do not detect the quasar with a 3 upper limit of 52 and 61 Jy at 4.67 and 7.67 GHz, respectively. As mentioned in , the measured flux density of this quasar is close to the detection limit of the LoTSS-DR2 survey at 144 MHz with a 3 tentative detection of 0.33 0.12 mJy beam ^ 1 and therefore J172+18 is likely a peak spectrum source.

Again, due to the limited number of radio detections, the model fits are not well constrained. Therefore, we can only conclude that the radio spectrum of J172+18 likely peaks between 0.25-1.3 GHz in the observed frame, which translates to 2-10 GHz in rest-frame. J172+18 can therefore be classified as a GPS source (as suggested also by Momjian2021AJ....161..207M ). Future GMRT band-3 and -4 observations could further constrain the peak frequency.

sources.summary: The paper reports J1129+1846 as a radio-loud quasar without successful Gaussian line fitting, undetected in [ C 2 ], dust-continuum faint, and excluded from kinematic analysis.

quotes.quote: For the object (J1129+1846) without a successful Gaussian fitting from the 1D spectra, we assume the line has the same redshift as that derived from the Mg 2 line and a width of FWHM = 300 km s^ 1 .

Out of 31 targeted objects, only four quasars (J0829+4117, J0839+3900, J1129+1846, and J1216+4519) are undetected in [ C 2 ] and two quasars have marginal line detections at a 2.6 level (J0213 0626) and a 2.1 level (J0837+4929), respectively.

All objects observed with ALMA, except for J1129+1846, are detected in the dust continuum at high significance. Similar to the [ C 2 ] results, the FIR continua of J0829+4117, J0839+3900, and J1216+4519 are not detected by NOEMA. J1129+1846, the only known radio-loud quasar in our sample, is intrinsically faint in both [ C 2 ] and FIR see also Khusanova22 .
